items: State your assumptions explicitly. If uncertain, ask.
If multiple interpretations exist, present them — don't pick silently.
If a simpler approach exists, say so. Push back when warranted.
If something is unclear, stop. Name what's confusing. Ask.
Analyze and understand the existing project architecture before making changes.
Respect existing conventions and design patterns; avoid introducing regressions or inconsistent abstractions.
No features beyond what was asked (YAGNI).
No abstractions for single-use code.
No "flexibility" or "configurability" that wasn't requested.
No error handling for impossible scenarios.
If you write 200 lines and it could be 50, rewrite it.

body.note: Ask yourself: "Would a senior engineer say this is overcomplicated?" If yes, simplify. Prefer simple, maintainable, predictable, and non-breaking solutions — KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid). Use reusable abstractions only when justified by real duplication or maintenance benefits — DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), SOLID. Never build for hypothetical future needs — YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)..

items: Don't "improve" adjacent code, comments, or formatting.
Don't refactor things that aren't broken.
Match existing style, even if you'd do it differently.
If you notice unrelated dead code, mention it — don't delete it.
Prefer incremental changes and maintain backward compatibility unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
Remove imports/variables/functions that YOUR changes made unused.
Don't remove pre-existing dead code unless asked.

body.note: The test: every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.

items: "Add validation" > "Write tests for invalid inputs, then make them pass"
"Fix the bug" > "Write a test that reproduces it, then make it pass"
"Refactor X" > "Ensure tests pass before and after"

body.note: For multi-step tasks, state a brief plan with explicit verify steps. Strong success criteria let you loop independently. Weak criteria ("make it work") require constant clarification.

items: Use RTK (Rust Token Kill) aggressively during vibe coding sessions to reduce unnecessary token usage.
Prefer concise, high-signal communication and implementation steps.
Keep modules focused, readable, and easy to maintain.
Centralize business logic in shared/core layers whenever possible.
Avoid duplicating validation, parsing, or transformation logic across layers.
Use RTK (Rust Token Kill) aggressively during vibe coding sessions to reduce unnecessary token usage and optimize development efficiency.
Before implementing any feature, always analyze and understand the existing project architecture to avoid regressions, inconsistent abstractions, or conflicting design patterns.

The project may also include other languages, tooling, scripting environments, or paradigms. Respect and follow the established conventions, architecture, and best practices of each language or ecosystem used in the repository.
`camelCase` for functions
`camelCase` for variables, fields, parameters, and local bindings
`kebab-case` for modules and filenames
`snake_case` for database tables and collections
`PascalCase` for structs, enums, traits, classes, and exported types
`SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` for constants and compile-time values

body.note: Vibe coding sessions: AI explanations and responses must be in French. Code, comments, commit messages, and docs remain in English without exception.

items: Write tests for: bug fixes, business logic, edge cases, public APIs, integration behavior.
Skip tests for: trivial getters/setters, pass-through wrappers, static config objects.
Bug fix workflow: reproduce > write failing test > implement fix > verify pass.
Style: FIRST principles · AAA structure · synchronous unless async is the subject · deterministic · tests close to source.
Mocking: mock external APIs, databases, filesystem. Don't mock internal utilities, standard libraries, or core framework behavior.
Before every commit: verify the current Git identity and correct any invalid configuration.
Analyze all changed files before committing.
Group related modifications into logical, coherent commits; separate unrelated changes into different commits.
Avoid oversized or mixed-purpose commits.
Prefer atomic commits with clear scope: `feature`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `config`, `test`.
Run tests and type checking.
Review for regressions and breaking changes.
Ensure consistency with existing architecture and conventions.
Update documentation when necessary.
Keep documentation close to the project source, concise, practical, and up to date.
Prefer maintainable and versioned documentation structures.
RTK (Rust Token Killer) wraps CLI commands to strip boilerplate and minimize token output.
If RTK has a subcommand for it, that subcommand is the only acceptable form. Raw commands are forbidden.
When unsure if RTK supports a command: run `rtk rewrite <cmd>` first — it returns the RTK equivalent.

rows: Token savings summary for current session
Find missed RTK opportunities in Claude Code history
Get the RTK equivalent of any raw command

items: Taskmaster is the primary tool for managing all project tasks. Use its MCP server in every vibe coding session.
Priority order: MCP server first > CLI fallback (`task-master -h`). Never manage tasks manually.

rows: Most important — full task details (description, status, dependencies, notes). Always run before starting a task.
Next recommended task based on dependency graph. Use when no ID is provided.

body.intro: If no task ID is provided, run `task-master next` first. Then, before writing any code:

items: Fetch task details via Taskmaster MCP (or `task-master show <id>` as fallback). Never start without reading full task details.
Identify relevant docs via `docs/index.md` — centralizes all project documentation. Fall back to listing `docs/` by name if needed.
Begin development only once requirements and documentation are sufficiently understood.

body.intro: When dealing with uncertain or framework-specific technical information:

items: Always use MCP Context7 as the first source of truth before answering or generating code.
Treat MCP Context7 as the authoritative runtime context for libraries, frameworks, and APIs.
Avoid relying on general or outdated knowledge when MCP Context7 is available.
The PocketBase MCP server is the mandatory interface for all PocketBase operations. Never use `curl`, raw HTTP, or the Admin UI for operations the MCP server can handle.
MCP PocketBase server — always first, for all operations.
PocketBase JS SDK — runtime app code only (hooks, frontend API calls).
Raw HTTP / curl — last resort only (e.g., auth collection user creation via admin API). Document why.
`POCKETBASE_URL` — PocketBase instance URL (required).
`POCKETBASE_ADMIN_EMAIL` / `POCKETBASE_ADMIN_PASSWORD` — needed for most operations.
Daily backups via Admin UI (`Settings > Backups`).
Retain >= 7 daily backups.
Optional: ship to S3-compatible storage (Backblaze B2 or similar).
Test restoration monthly in staging.
Generates TypeScript types from the PocketBase schema. Output: `pocketbase-types.ts`.
Never write manual collection types — always regenerate.
Run with: `bun run typegen` or `npx pocketbase-typegen --env`
Required `.env` vars: `PB_TYPEGEN_URL`, `PB_TYPEGEN_EMAIL`, `PB_TYPEGEN_PASSWORD`.
`package.json` script: `"typegen": "pocketbase-typegen --env"`

typed-instance.name: TypedPocketBase (recommended)
typed-instance.role: Cast pb as TypedPocketBase for auto-typed access per collection

explicit-generics.name: Explicit Generics
explicit-generics.role: Use Collections enum and Response types for explicit type annotations

expanding-relations.name: Expanding Relations
expanding-relations.role: Type Expand as { field: RelatedResponse } and pass via getOne options

typed-create-update.name: Typed Create / Update
typed-create-update.role: Use Create<Collections.X> and Update<Collections.X> for write operations

rows: Const object mapping collection names (PascalCase > ID)
Union type for select fields with defined values
Typed PocketBase instance for use with as TypedPocketBase

items: Start in Plan mode (read-only) to understand and validate the approach before touching code.
Describe your understanding of the task, ask clarifying questions.
Sequence the work steps before execution.
Switch to Build mode only after the plan is validated.
Quick questions or trivial changes do not need Plan mode.

body.note: Plan mode avoids costly back-and-forth caused by premature execution based on misunderstanding.

body.intro: Keep context lean at all times:

items: Monitor the token counter / context window percentage in the TUI.
Run /compact when context is ~50% full to summarize the session.
Compact regularly on long sessions, do not wait until saturation.
Each file read and tool output adds to context — be intentional.

body.note: Regular /compact reduces token consumption drastically without losing conversation continuity.

body.intro: Every active MCP server registers its tools in the model context on every message:

items: Activate only the MCP servers needed for the current task.
Deactivate them once the task is done.
A read-only doc server can stay active. A DB server should be on-demand only.
Ask: 'Would I give this tool unsupervised access to my system?' before enabling.

body.note: An active but unused MCP server continuously wastes tokens.

body.intro: Use sub-agents to isolate context and reduce token consumption:

items: Each sub-agent starts with a clean context — no accumulation from the main agent.
The main orchestrator stays lean, delegating details to sub-agents.
Use a read-only reviewer sub-agent to audit code without modification ability.
Define sub-agents in ~/.config/opencode/agents/ or .opencode/agents/.
Reference them via @mention in messages or auto-invocation.

body.note: Sub-agents prevent the main context from being polluted with thousands of lines of implementation detail.

body.intro: Control what the agent sees to avoid ingesting irrelevant files:

items: Exclude node_modules/, target/, dist/, *.lock, .git/.
Exclude large binary assets: images, videos, archives, WASM files.
Exclude logs, cache directories, and build artifacts.
Only include source files needed for the task.
Configure these in opencode.json or .opencodeignore.

body.note: Every unnecessary file ingested burns tokens with zero value.

body.intro: Session discipline directly impacts token efficiency:

items: Open a new session for each distinct task or topic.
A long session with mixed topics = cluttered context = rising costs.
Code consumes far more tokens than equivalent text — keep sessions focused.
Close and open a new session when switching subjects.

body.note: A clean session per task keeps context lean and relevant.

body.intro: Package recurring prompts into reusable commands:

items: Define custom commands in opencode.json to avoid retyping the same instructions.
Each command has a description and a calibrated prompt.
Example: a 'review' command that checks security, perf, error handling, test coverage.
Custom commands save tokens by replacing long repetitive prompts with short names.

body.note: A short, pre-calibrated prompt replaces reformulating a long instruction each session.
